other_observers: Someone who gave his name only as “Stan.” Apparently he frequents Chatfield and is a photographer.; Bird was found by "Stan."
specific_location: Chatfield State Park, along the main road about 50 feet west of Kingfisher Bridge
description: The following is a verbatim excerpt from my original paper writeup, dated March 13, 2003:  Structure:  The bird was chicken-like in structure, clearly a member of the Galliformes. It gave the impression of being large for a grouse. Its head showed a slight crest and its tail was medium-length and squared-off. During the observation, I specifically noted its feathered tarsi.  Plumage:  Caveat: although I took notes immediately after this encounter, I subsequently lost them, and must reconstruct my observation from memory.  The plumage was mottled and fairly varied. Overall it was rather dark gray. On the breast and foreneck there were some patches of the “blue” color characteristic of male Blue Grouse, which is not really blue but a kind of pale slaty gray. However, this color was not widespread on the bird. The upperside of the bird had numerous bright white spots, formed by white patches on parts of the webbing of individual feathers. These white spots are not illustrated in Sibley or any other field guide I have; however, I have had subsequent opportunities to verify their existence on other Blue Grouse I have seen in Colorado. Likewise, Sibley does not show any birds with as little blue in the plumage as I observed; this leads me to surmise that the bird might have been a young male, but that is pure guesswork on my part. These are the only solid details I can recall at this time.Behaviors: The following is a verbatim excerpt from my original paper writeup, dated March 13, 2003:  Observation time, distance, lighting and optics:  The bird was discovered by Stan before I arrived. I stopped when I saw him parked in the entrance to the parking lot north of the park road and just west of Kingfisher Bridge, because he was staring into the woods with binoculars. When I asked him what birds he had seen, he informed me that a probable Blue Grouse had just walked across the road and into the adjacent trees. Together we followed the bird into the shrubbery, and sure enough, we found a Blue Grouse pacing along the ground. I was able to observe the bird at 10-15 feet distance for at least a couple of minutes. As is typical of the species, it was only slightly wary, and responded to close approach by walking away rather unhurriedly. In this fashion we followed it back across the park road and lost it in a small patch of brush on the south side. Starting fifteen minutes later, Andrew Spencer and Joey Kellner and I tried to relocate it, but we were ultimately unsuccessful.Call: Bird was silent.Plumage: possibly molting into adult
